flashcards.back: Thrashing occurs when the system spends more time swapping pages between memory and disk than executing useful work, degrading performance.
A phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames results in more page faults, seen in FIFO page replacement.

flashcards.front: What is the working set model?
flashcards.back: A strategy that determines which pages a process is actively using to prevent thrashing by allocating adequate frames.

flashcards.front: What is the optimal page replacement algorithm?
flashcards.back: Theoretical algorithm that replaces the page not used for longest future time. Impossible to implement practically.

flashcards.front: How does LRU differ from FIFO?
flashcards.back: LRU considers recent usage history while FIFO only considers insertion order. LRU avoids Belady's anomaly.
Interrupt occurring when a program accesses a page not currently mapped to physical memory.

flashcards.front: What is the clock (second chance) algorithm?
flashcards.back: Page replacement using circular buffer with reference bit. Pages with bit=0 are replaced first.
Insufficient physical memory for active processes, leading to constant page swapping.
Use working set model, increase physical memory, or employ local replacement algorithms.
Loading pages into memory only when needed, reducing initial load time and memory usage.
Flag indicating if a page has been modified. Unmodified pages don't need writing to disk during replacement.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of a page table?
flashcards.back: Maps virtual addresses to physical addresses, tracking page locations and access permissions.
